summary:
The story is narrated by Nick Carraway, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg, Long Island in 1922 to learn the bond business. Nick reflects on advice from his father about reserving judgment, which has made him a confidant to many people. He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, whom he describes with both scorn and fascination, noting Gatsby's extraordinary gift for hope and sensitivity to life's promises.

Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their elaborate Georgian mansion in fashionable East Egg. Tom, a former Yale football star, is physically imposing but seems to have peaked in college. During dinner with Daisy, Tom, and the athletic Jordan Baker, tensions emerge - Tom espouses racist theories about Nordic superiority, Daisy displays a bruised knuckle apparently caused by Tom, and a mysterious phone call interrupts the evening. The dinner reveals the hollow sophistication of the wealthy East Coast elite, setting up the social world that will drive the story's conflicts.

Gatsby reveals he was also in the war, serving in a different division than Nick.

summary:
The story is narrated by Nick Carraway, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg, Long Island in 1922 to learn the bond business. He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson, a garage owner's wife from the valley of ashes. After accompanying Tom to meet Myrtle and witnessing a violent confrontation where Tom breaks her nose, Nick attends one of Gatsby's extravagant parties and finally meets his enigmatic host - an elegant young man with an extraordinary smile who claims to be an Oxford-educated war veteran.

At the party, Jordan Baker has a mysterious private conversation with Gatsby lasting about an hour, after which she tells Nick she's heard "the most amazing thing" but refuses to reveal what it was. Nick begins a relationship with Jordan despite discovering her fundamental dishonesty, including cheating at golf and lying about a car accident. Meanwhile, Nick settles into his New York routine, working at the Probity Trust and experiencing both the excitement and loneliness of city life. Gatsby's parties continue throughout the summer, attended by a colorful array of wealthy, corrupt, and notorious guests from both eggs of Long Island. When Gatsby finally visits Nick personally, he becomes nervous and begins telling an elaborate story about his background - claiming to be from a wealthy Middle Western family (though he says San Francisco), educated at Oxford, and having lived "like a young rajah" collecting jewels and hunting big game across Europe while trying to forget some past tragedy.

summary:
The story is narrated by Nick Carraway, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg, Long Island in 1922 to learn the bond business. He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson. After witnessing violence at Tom's apartment where he breaks Myrtle's nose, Nick attends one of Gatsby's parties and meets his enigmatic host.

Jordan Baker has a mysterious conversation with Gatsby, and Nick begins a relationship with her despite recognizing her dishonesty. When Gatsby visits Nick, he tells elaborate stories about his background - claiming to be from a wealthy Middle Western family, Oxford-educated, and a war hero. He produces seemingly authentic evidence including a medal from Montenegro and an Oxford photograph. Gatsby then takes Nick to New York for lunch, where they meet Meyer Wolfshiem, a shady gambler who fixed the 1919 World Series. The meeting reveals Gatsby's connection to criminal elements and his mysterious "business on the side." Jordan later reveals the truth: Gatsby and Daisy were lovers five years ago in Louisville, and Gatsby has bought his mansion specifically to be near her. He wants Nick to arrange a reunion by inviting Daisy to tea, having waited five years and thrown countless parties hoping she might attend. The green light Gatsby reaches toward is revealed to be at the end of Daisy's dock, symbolizing his longing for their lost love.

summary:
The story is narrated by Nick Carraway, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg, Long Island in 1922 to learn the bond business. He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson. After witnessing violence at Tom's apartment where he breaks Myrtle's nose, Nick attends one of Gatsby's parties and meets his enigmatic host.

Jordan Baker reveals that Gatsby and Daisy were lovers five years ago in Louisville, and Gatsby has bought his mansion specifically to be near her. He wants Nick to arrange a reunion by inviting Daisy to tea, having waited five years and thrown countless parties hoping she might attend. The green light Gatsby reaches toward is revealed to be at the end of Daisy's dock, symbolizing his longing for their lost love. Nick arranges the tea meeting at his house on a rainy afternoon. Initially awkward and painfully embarrassed, Gatsby and Daisy's reunion transforms into joy once left alone. Gatsby shows Daisy his mansion, and she is overwhelmed by his wealth, breaking down in tears over his beautiful shirts. As they reconnect, the green light loses its symbolic power now that Daisy is real and present. The narrator reveals Gatsby's true identity: James Gatz from North Dakota, who reinvented himself at seventeen when he met the wealthy Dan Cody on Lake Superior, transforming from a poor farm boy into the idealized Jay Gatsby through sheer force of imagination and will.

Successfully arranges and hosts the reunion between Gatsby and Daisy at his house, tactfully leaving them alone together.

After dismissing his servants, Gatsby ends his career as party host and the house goes dark on Saturday nights.

places.name: Long Island
places.description: Island extending east of New York containing the two 'egg' formations and Long Island Sound. Subject to rumors that Gatsby lives in a boat that moves secretly along its shore.

places.name: Yale Club
places.description: New York club where Nick regularly dines, though he finds it the gloomiest event of his day. Has a library where Nick studies investments.

places.name: Probity Trust
places.description: Financial firm in lower New York where Nick works as a bond salesman with other clerks.

places.name: New York City
places.description: The city where Nick works and experiences both excitement and loneliness. He enjoys its 'racy, adventurous feel' at night and walking Fifth Avenue, but also feels the haunting loneliness of metropolitan twilight. Gatsby and Nick have lunch here at a restaurant near the old Metropole. Electric trains carry commuters home through the rain. On the hottest day of summer, the heat is oppressive and affects everyone's mood and behavior.

places.name: Oxford
places.description: English university where Gatsby claims to have been educated. He produces a seemingly authentic photograph from Trinity Quad showing him with other students, one now the Earl of Doncaster.

places.name: San Francisco
places.description: City Gatsby identifies as his Middle Western hometown, revealing his geographical confusion or deception.

places.name: Louisville
places.description: Kentucky city where Daisy grew up and first met Gatsby in 1917 when he was a young officer and she was eighteen. She was the most popular young girl there, dressed in white with a white roadster. Site of her elaborate wedding to Tom Buchanan. Gatsby dreams of returning there with Daisy to be married from her house, recreating their past exactly as it was five years ago. Scene of Gatsby's romantic memory of kissing Daisy on a moonlit sidewalk in autumn.

places.name: Queensboro Bridge
places.description: Bridge connecting Queens to Manhattan where Nick experiences the magical promise of the city. From here, the city appears 'built with a wish out of non-olfactory money' with its mystery and beauty.

places.name: The Old Metropole
places.description: Former restaurant where gangster Rosy Rosenthal was murdered. Meyer Wolfshiem tells Nick the story of how Rosenthal was shot three times after being lured outside. The restaurant is 'filled with faces dead and gone' and holds memories of the criminal underworld.

places.name: Plaza Hotel
places.description: Upscale New York hotel where Jordan Baker tells Nick the story of Daisy and Gatsby's past romance over tea in the tea-garden.

places.name: Nick's House
places.description: Small rented house in West Egg next to Gatsby's mansion. Site of the carefully arranged reunion between Gatsby and Daisy on a rainy afternoon. Has a living room, kitchen, pantry, and lawn that Gatsby has cut. Nick takes shelter under a large black knotted tree in the back during the rain.

places.name: Lake Superior
places.description: Great Lake where seventeen-year-old James Gatz worked as a clam-digger and salmon-fisher before meeting Dan Cody. Site of Gatz's transformation into Jay Gatsby when he warned Cody about dangerous winds and was taken aboard the yacht Tuolomee.

places.name: North Dakota
places.description: James Gatz's true birthplace, home to his 'shiftless and unsuccessful farm people' parents whom his imagination never really accepted.

places.name: St. Olaf's College
places.description: Small Lutheran college in southern Minnesota where James Gatz stayed for two weeks before leaving, dismayed by its indifference to his destiny and despising the janitor's work he was supposed to do to pay his way.

places.name: West Indies
places.description: One of the destinations where Dan Cody's yacht Tuolomee traveled during Gatsby's five years of service, along with the Barbary Coast.

places.name: Barbary Coast
places.description: One of the destinations where Dan Cody's yacht traveled during the five years Gatsby worked for him. The boat went three times around the Continent during this period.

places.name: Boston
places.description: City where Ella Kaye came aboard Dan Cody's yacht, and where Cody died a week later, ending Gatsby's five-year employment and education in wealth.

places.name: Duluth
places.description: City where Dan Cody took young James Gatz to buy him a blue coat, six pairs of white duck trousers, and a yachting cap, completing his transformation into Jay Gatsby.

places.name: Little Girl Bay
places.description: Bay on Lake Superior where Dan Cody's yacht anchored and where he first encountered seventeen-year-old James Gatz, changing the young man's destiny forever.

places.name: Buchanan House
places.description: Tom and Daisy's mansion in East Egg with a hall, salon, and crimson carpet. Shadowed with awnings and cooled by fans during the oppressive summer heat. Site of the tense lunch meeting between Nick, Gatsby, Daisy, Jordan, and Tom on the hottest day of summer.

open_threads.description: The symbolic presence of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg's eyes watching over the moral wasteland of the valley of ashes
Nick's romantic relationship with Jordan Baker despite his recognition of her fundamental dishonesty and his need to resolve a relationship back home
Gatsby's mysterious business dealings and offer to Nick of a 'confidential' side opportunity that would provide good money, suggesting involvement in illegal activities
The phone call Gatsby receives during Daisy's visit, discussing business about 'small towns' and Detroit, suggesting ongoing criminal activities that threaten to intrude on his romantic reunion
Growing media attention and rumors about Gatsby, including a reporter's visit and legends about underground pipelines to Canada, suggesting his notoriety is becoming dangerous
Tom's growing suspicion of Gatsby, calling him a bootlegger and expressing determination to find out who he is and what he does, while also being perturbed by Daisy's behavior
Gatsby's impossible dream to repeat the past exactly as it was, wanting Daisy to deny she ever loved Tom so they can return to Louisville and marry from her house as if five years hadn't passed
The end of Gatsby's parties and his dismissal of servants to prevent gossip about Daisy's afternoon visits, replacing them with Wolfshiem's criminal associates

Rising tensions and the oppressive atmosphere building toward a confrontation, as evidenced by the hottest day of summer and the tense lunch at the Buchanans' house where Daisy openly shows affection for Gatsby

summary:
The story is narrated by Nick Carraway, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg, Long Island in 1922 to learn the bond business. He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson. After witnessing violence at Tom's apartment where he breaks Myrtle's nose, Nick attends one of Gatsby's parties and meets his enigmatic host.

Jordan Baker reveals that Gatsby and Daisy were lovers five years ago in Louisville, and Gatsby has bought his mansion specifically to be near her. Nick arranges a reunion, and after an initially awkward tea meeting, Gatsby and Daisy reconnect joyfully. The narrator reveals Gatsby's true identity: James Gatz from North Dakota, who reinvented himself at seventeen when he met the wealthy Dan Cody. After several weeks apart, tensions grow when Tom visits Gatsby's mansion and learns of his connection to Daisy. At Gatsby's next party, Daisy is appalled by the raw vulgarity, devastating Gatsby who dreams impossibly of recreating their past exactly as it was five years ago. On the hottest day of summer, the confrontation comes to a head when the group drives to New York City, where Gatsby and Tom fight over Daisy. Tom reveals Gatsby's criminal associations, and though Daisy initially declares her love for Gatsby, she cannot deny that she once loved Tom, shattering Gatsby's dream.

Returning from the city, tragedy strikes when Daisy, driving Gatsby's yellow car, hits and kills Myrtle Wilson who rushes into the road thinking Tom is driving. Myrtle's husband George, who had discovered her affair and locked her up planning to move away, is devastated by her death. Tom tells George that Gatsby was the driver, and George shoots Gatsby dead while he floats in his pool. Nick, disgusted by the moral emptiness of the wealthy East Coast elite, arranges Gatsby's funeral and returns to the Midwest, reflecting on Gatsby's incorruptible dream and the corruption of the American Dream itself.

people.name: Nick Carraway
people.description: The narrator, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg in 1922 to learn the bond business. Yale graduate, WWI veteran, works at Probity Trust in lower New York. Successfully arranged the reunion between Gatsby and Daisy. Becomes increasingly disgusted with the moral emptiness of the wealthy elite. Present at the garage after Myrtle's death, feels sick and wants to be alone. Discovers the truth about the accident from Gatsby and spies on Tom and Daisy conspiring together. Stays up all night worrying about Gatsby and warns him to leave town. Listens to Gatsby's story about his past with Daisy and his impossible dream.

people.name: Jay Gatsby
people.description: Nick's mysterious wealthy neighbor whose real name is James Gatz from North Dakota. Desperately in love with Daisy and dreams of recreating their past exactly as it was. Owns a yellow car that kills Myrtle Wilson while Daisy is driving it. Takes responsibility for the accident to protect Daisy and stands vigil outside her house all night, watching over nothing. Refuses to leave town despite danger. Reveals the full story of his youth and romance with Daisy to Nick - how he was a penniless soldier who took Daisy under false pretenses but genuinely fell in love. Still believes Daisy never loved Tom and that their afternoon encounter was 'just personal.'

people.name: Daisy Buchanan
people.description: Nick's second cousin, married to Tom. The first 'nice' girl Gatsby ever knew, whom he met as a young officer at Camp Taylor in Louisville. Lives in a beautiful house that represented wealth and mystery to the young Gatsby. After Gatsby went to war, she felt pressure from the world outside and married Tom Buchanan for his 'wholesome bulkiness' and position. Was driving Gatsby's yellow car when it killed Myrtle Wilson but loses her nerve afterward. Locks herself in her room but later sits conspiring with Tom over cold chicken and ale, showing natural intimacy despite the tragedy. Described as extraordinary, with youth and mystery that wealth imprisons and preserves.

people.name: Tom Buchanan
people.description: Daisy's wealthy husband, former Yale football star. Physically imposing and arrogant with a mistress named Myrtle Wilson. Present at the garage after Myrtle's death and shows emotion, calling the driver a coward. Lies to George Wilson, claiming he just arrived from New York and that the yellow car wasn't his. Takes charge at the accident scene and carries Wilson inside. Later conspires intimately with Daisy at their kitchen table, showing their natural partnership despite not being happy. Represents the 'wholesome bulkiness' and social position that attracted Daisy when Gatsby was stuck at Oxford after the war.

people.name: Jordan Baker
people.description: Athletic young woman, famous golf champion and friend of Daisy's. Has a romantic relationship with Nick but he becomes disgusted with her along with all the others. Too wise to carry well-forgotten dreams from age to age, unlike Daisy. Present at the garage after the accident and pushed inside with Nick to see Myrtle's body. Her face falls against Nick's shoulder as they drive home, and the pressure of her hand reassures him about turning thirty.

people.name: Myrtle Wilson
people.description: Tom Buchanan's mistress, wife of garage owner George Wilson. Killed when she runs into the road toward Gatsby's yellow car, thinking Tom is driving. Had been locked up by her husband George who discovered her affair and planned to move away. Her final words were shouting at George to beat her and calling him a coward. Dies instantly with her left breast torn loose and thick dark blood mingling with the dust. Her body is wrapped in blankets and laid on a worktable in the garage.

people.name: George Wilson
people.description: Owner of a garage in the valley of ashes. Myrtle's husband who discovered her affair and locked her upstairs, planning to move away. Pale as his own hair and shaking, he appeared sick before the accident. After Myrtle's death, he stands swaying in his office doorway, holding the doorposts and repeatedly crying 'Oh, my Ga-od!' Knows what kind of car killed his wife - a yellow car. Tom carries him like a doll and sits with him after the accident.

people.name: Michaelis
people.description: Young Greek man who runs the coffee joint beside the ash-heaps. Principal witness at the inquest into Myrtle's death. Found George Wilson sick in his office and tried to persuade him to go to bed. Witnessed Myrtle rushing out into the dusk and the accident. Reached Myrtle first along with another driver and saw her fatal injuries. Tells police there were two cars, one coming from New York that hit her going thirty or forty miles per hour.

people.name: Dan Cody
people.description: Wealthy yacht owner who became Gatsby's mentor and 'best friend.' His photograph hangs over Gatsby's desk. Transformed James Gatz into Jay Gatsby and employed him for five years. Gatsby tells Nick the full story of his time with Cody on the night after Myrtle's death, explaining how Cody shaped his identity and dreams of wealth.

places.name: West Egg
places.description: Less fashionable of two egg-shaped land formations on Long Island, where Nick rents a small house and Gatsby owns his mansion. Site of Gatsby's elaborate parties that represented 'spectroscopic gaiety.' Nick returns here after the accident and finds Gatsby standing vigil outside the Buchanan house.

places.name: East Egg
places.description: More fashionable area across the bay from West Egg, where Tom and Daisy Buchanan live. Their house floats through the dark trees with lighted windows among the vines. Site where Gatsby keeps his vigil and where Tom and Daisy conspire together after Myrtle's death.

places.name: Valley of Ashes
places.description: Desolate wasteland between West Egg and New York where George Wilson's garage is located. Site of Myrtle Wilson's death when she runs into the road and is struck by Gatsby's yellow car. The garage becomes a scene of tragedy with crowds gathering and Wilson's anguished cries echoing through the night.

places.name: Wilson's Garage
places.description: George Wilson's automobile repair shop in the valley of ashes where the tragedy unfolds. Wilson had locked Myrtle upstairs before her death. After the accident, her body lies wrapped in blankets on a worktable, while Wilson sways in his office doorway crying out in anguish. Lit only by a yellow light in a swinging metal basket, it becomes the center of police investigation.

places.name: Gatsby's Mansion
places.description: Enormous mansion in West Egg that seems especially large and empty on the night after Myrtle's death. Nick and Gatsby search through great rooms for cigarettes, pushing aside curtains like pavilions. The house is dusty and musty as if not aired for days, with a ghostly piano and French windows opening onto darkness. Gatsby's front door remains open as he returns from his vigil.

places.name: Long Island
places.description: Island extending east of New York where the tragic events unfold. Dawn breaks over Long Island as Nick and Gatsby open windows, filling the house with grey-turning, gold-turning light. The shadow of trees fall across the dew and ghostly birds sing among blue leaves, promising a cool, lovely day after the night of tragedy.

places.name: Camp Taylor
places.description: Military camp in Louisville where Gatsby was stationed as a young officer and first met Daisy. He initially visited her house with other officers, then alone. The camp with its tents represented his humble circumstances compared to Daisy's beautiful house and wealthy world.

places.name: Louisville
places.description: Kentucky city where Daisy grew up and first met Gatsby when he was a young officer in 1917. Site of their romance and Daisy's beautiful house that amazed the young Gatsby. After the war, Daisy moved through its social world of orchids, orchestras, and evening parties before marrying Tom Buchanan. The artificial world was redolent of cheerful snobbery and the rhythm of the year.

places.name: Oxford
places.description: English university where Gatsby was sent after the Argonne battles due to some complication or misunderstanding, when he desperately wanted to return home to Daisy. His delay at Oxford contributed to Daisy's decision to marry Tom Buchanan, as she felt pressure from the world outside and wanted her life shaped immediately.

places.name: Buchanan House
places.description: Tom and Daisy's mansion in East Egg where Gatsby keeps vigil after Myrtle's death. Has a second floor with windows that bloom with light among the vines. Contains a kitchen where Tom and Daisy sit conspiring together over cold fried chicken and ale, showing natural intimacy. The drawing-room has open curtains and there's a pantry window where Nick spies on them.

open_threads.description: The symbolic presence of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg's eyes watching over the moral wasteland of the valley of ashes

The aftermath of Myrtle Wilson's death and its consequences for all involved - George Wilson's grief and quest for justice, Gatsby's protection of Daisy despite the danger, and the moral reckoning that follows
Tom and Daisy's conspiracy and natural intimacy revealed as they sit together after the tragedy, showing their partnership in protecting themselves from consequences
Gatsby's impossible dream finally shattered but his refusal to accept reality - he still believes Daisy never loved Tom and clings to hope despite the danger to his life
Nick's growing disgust and moral awakening as he becomes sickened by the wealthy elite's behavior and their ability to retreat into their carelessness, leaving others to clean up the mess

He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson. Jordan Baker reveals that Gatsby and Daisy were lovers five years ago in Louisville, and Gatsby has bought his mansion specifically to be near her. Nick arranges a reunion, and after an initially awkward tea meeting, Gatsby and Daisy reconnect joyfully. The narrator reveals Gatsby's true identity: James Gatz from North Dakota, who reinvented himself at seventeen when he met the wealthy Dan Cody.

On the hottest day of summer, tensions explode when Gatsby and Tom fight over Daisy in New York City. Though Daisy initially declares her love for Gatsby, she cannot deny that she once loved Tom, shattering Gatsby's dream of recreating their past exactly as it was. Returning from the city, tragedy strikes when Daisy, driving Gatsby's yellow car, hits and kills Myrtle Wilson who rushes into the road thinking Tom is driving. George Wilson, devastated by his wife's death and believing the driver was also her lover, seeks vengeance. Nick discovers the bodies and becomes solely responsible for Gatsby's funeral arrangements as everyone else abandons him. Daisy and Tom flee without leaving an address, Meyer Wolfshiem refuses to get involved despite their criminal partnership, and even party guests like Klipspringer show no genuine concern. Only Gatsby's father, Henry Gatz, arrives from Minnesota to mourn his son. Nick, disgusted by the moral emptiness and carelessness of the wealthy elite, reflects on Gatsby's incorruptible dream and the corruption of the American Dream itself.

people.name: Nick Carraway
people.description: The narrator, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg in 1922 to learn the bond business. Yale graduate, WWI veteran. Becomes the sole person responsible for Gatsby's funeral arrangements and affairs after his death. Calls Daisy, Wolfshiem, and others seeking help but finds himself abandoned by all of Gatsby's former associates. Searches through Gatsby's desk and handles all practical matters. Feels a 'scornful solidarity' with Gatsby against the world that has abandoned him. Disgusted by the moral emptiness of the wealthy elite.

people.name: Jay Gatsby
people.description: Nick's mysterious wealthy neighbor whose real name is James Gatz from North Dakota. Desperately in love with Daisy and dreams of recreating their past exactly as it was. Never used his pool all summer until the day he dies. Spends his final morning with Nick, hoping Daisy will call. Shot and killed by George Wilson while floating on a pneumatic mattress in his pool, still clinging to hope that Daisy would contact him. His father Henry Gatz describes him as having 'a lot of brain power' and destined to be 'a great man like James J. Hill.'

people.name: Daisy Buchanan
people.description: Nick's second cousin, married to Tom. The first 'nice' girl Gatsby ever knew, whom he met as a young officer at Camp Taylor in Louisville. After the accident that kills Myrtle Wilson, she flees with Tom, taking baggage and leaving no forwarding address. Abandons Gatsby completely after his death - never calls, sends no wire, and provides no support during his funeral arrangements. Her house was the site where Gatsby kept his final vigil, hoping she would call.

people.name: Tom Buchanan
people.description: Daisy's wealthy husband, former Yale football star. Physically imposing and arrogant. After Myrtle Wilson's death, he flees with Daisy taking baggage and leaving no forwarding address. Completely abandons any responsibility for the tragedy he helped precipitate by telling George Wilson that Gatsby was the driver of the yellow car. Shows the carelessness of the wealthy elite who retreat into their vast wealth and let others clean up their messes.

people.name: Jordan Baker
people.description: Athletic young woman, famous golf champion and friend of Daisy's. Has a romantic relationship with Nick that ends after Gatsby's death. Calls Nick from Hempstead the morning after the tragedy, but their conversation becomes strained and hostile. They speak coldly and abruptly hang up on each other, marking the end of their relationship. Nick becomes too disgusted with her and all the others to continue the association.

people.name: George Wilson
people.description: Owner of a garage in the valley of ashes. Myrtle's husband who discovered her affair and locked her upstairs before her death. Spends the night after the accident with Michaelis, rocking back and forth and muttering incoherently. Becomes convinced that the driver of the yellow car was also his wife's lover. Finds an expensive dog leash among Myrtle's possessions, further confirming his suspicions. Disappears from the garage and tracks down Gatsby, shooting him dead before killing himself. Described by police as 'deranged by grief.'

people.name: Myrtle Wilson
people.description: Tom Buchanan's mistress, wife of garage owner George Wilson. Killed when she runs into the road toward Gatsby's yellow car, thinking Tom is driving. Had been locked up by her husband George who discovered her affair and planned to move away. Wilson found an expensive dog leash wrapped in tissue paper on her bureau, evidence of her affair. Her sister Catherine protects her reputation at the inquest, swearing she never knew Gatsby and was completely happy with her husband.

people.name: Michaelis
people.description: Young Greek man who runs the coffee joint beside the ash-heaps. Principal witness at the inquest into Myrtle's death. Stays with George Wilson through the night after the accident, trying to comfort him and keep him calm. Witnesses Wilson's growing obsession with finding the yellow car's owner and his discovery of the dog leash. Makes coffee and tries to distract Wilson with questions about his marriage and church. Present when Wilson stares at the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg and declares 'God sees everything.'

people.name: Henry C. Gatz
people.description: Gatsby's father from Minnesota, a solemn old man who arrives after seeing news of his son's death in the Chicago newspaper. Helpless and dismayed, bundled in a cheap coat against the September weather. His eyes leak continuously with excitement and grief. Refers to his son as 'Jimmy' and is awed by the splendor of Gatsby's mansion. Believes his son would have been 'a great man like James J. Hill' and insists on keeping the body in the East where Jimmy 'rose up to his position.'

people.name: Meyer Wolfshiem
people.description: Gatsby's business associate involved in criminal activities. When Nick reaches out for help with funeral arrangements, Wolfshiem sends a cold letter refusing to get involved, claiming he's 'tied up in some very important business' and 'cannot get mixed up in this thing now.' His abandonment of Gatsby in death reveals the shallow, self-serving nature of their criminal partnership. Admits he doesn't know Gatsby's family at all.

people.name: Klipspringer
people.description: Frequent guest at Gatsby's parties who practically lived at the mansion. Calls Nick after Gatsby's death but shows no genuine concern for attending the funeral. An 'obviously frightened person' who seems reluctant to be associated with Gatsby now that he's dead. Represents the fair-weather friends who enjoyed Gatsby's hospitality but abandoned him in death.

people.name: Catherine
people.description: Myrtle Wilson's sister who arrives at the garage drunk the night of the accident and faints when she learns about the death. At the inquest, she shows surprising character and loyalty, swearing that her sister never knew Gatsby, was completely happy with her husband, and 'had been into no mischief whatever.' Her testimony helps keep the case simple by protecting her sister's reputation.

people.name: Dan Cody
people.description: Wealthy yacht owner who became Gatsby's mentor and 'best friend.' His photograph hangs over Gatsby's desk and is the only personal item Nick finds when searching through Gatsby's papers after his death. Described as 'a token of forgotten violence, staring down from the wall.' Represents Gatsby's transformation from James Gatz and his introduction to wealth.

places.name: West Egg
places.description: Less fashionable of two egg-shaped land formations on Long Island, where Nick rents a small house and Gatsby owns his mansion. Site of Gatsby's death in his pool and the subsequent investigation. George Wilson arrives here asking for directions to Gatsby's house, having discovered his identity. The scene of the final tragedy and Gatsby's funeral arrangements.

places.name: East Egg
places.description: More fashionable area across the bay from West Egg, where Tom and Daisy Buchanan live. Tom and Daisy flee from here the afternoon of Gatsby's death, taking baggage and leaving no forwarding address. The site of their final conspiracy and abandonment of responsibility for the tragedy.

places.name: Valley of Ashes
places.description: Desolate wasteland between West Egg and New York where George Wilson's garage is located and Myrtle Wilson died. The site of Wilson's all-night vigil with Michaelis, where he plots his revenge and stares at the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg. Wilson begins his journey of vengeance from here, tracking down Gatsby's identity.

places.name: Wilson's Garage
places.description: George Wilson's automobile repair shop in the valley of ashes where Wilson spends the night after Myrtle's death, rocking back and forth on the couch in his office. The work bench is stained where Myrtle's body had been lying. Wilson shows Michaelis the expensive dog leash he found among his wife's possessions. The starting point of Wilson's quest for revenge.

places.name: Gatsby's Pool
places.description: Swimming pool at Gatsby's mansion where he spends his final day on a pneumatic mattress, waiting for a phone call that never comes. The site of his death when George Wilson shoots him. His body floats on the mattress, tracing 'a thin red circle in the water' as it drifts. Gatsby had never used the pool all summer until the day he died.

places.name: Gatsby's Mansion
places.description: Enormous mansion in West Egg that becomes a crime scene after Gatsby's murder. Police, photographers, and newspapermen swarm the house. Nick searches through Gatsby's desk finding only Dan Cody's photograph. Henry Gatz is awed by the height and splendor of the hall and great rooms. The site of the funeral preparations and the final abandonment by Gatsby's fair-weather friends.

places.name: Louisville
places.description: Kentucky city where Daisy grew up and first met Gatsby when he was a young officer in 1917. Gatsby returns there after the war on the last of his army pay, walking the streets where their footsteps had clicked together and revisiting places they had driven in her white car. He leaves feeling he might have found her if he had searched harder, stretching out his hand desperately as the train carries him away from where 'she had drawn her breath.'

places.name: Port Roosevelt
places.description: Location where George Wilson's movements were traced on his journey to find Gatsby. Part of his methodical search for the owner of the yellow car on the day he commits the murders.

places.name: Gad's Hill
places.description: Town where George Wilson stops during his journey to find Gatsby, arriving at noon. He buys a sandwich he doesn't eat and a cup of coffee. Boys see him 'acting sort of crazy' and motorists notice him staring oddly from the roadside. After three unaccounted hours, he continues to West Egg.

places.name: Hempstead
places.description: Location where Jordan Baker calls Nick from the morning after Gatsby's death. She had left Daisy's house and was planning to go to Southampton, but their relationship ends during the cold, hostile phone conversation.

places.name: Minnesota
places.description: State where Henry C. Gatz lives and from which he sends a telegram postponing the funeral until he can arrive. Represents the authentic American heartland in contrast to the corrupt East Coast.

open_threads.description: The symbolic presence of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg's eyes watching over the moral wasteland of the valley of ashes, culminating in Wilson's declaration that 'God sees everything' as he stares at the billboard
The aftermath of Myrtle Wilson's death and its consequences - George Wilson's quest for vengeance leading to the murder of Gatsby and his own suicide, completing the tragic cycle
Tom and Daisy's careless abandonment of responsibility as they flee after the tragedy, leaving others to clean up the mess they helped create
Gatsby's impossible dream and his final tragic hope that Daisy would call, dying still clinging to his illusions about recapturing the past
Nick's moral awakening and disgust with the wealthy elite's behavior, leading to his complete break with Jordan and his sole responsibility for Gatsby's funeral as everyone else abandons him
The funeral arrangements for Gatsby and the revelation of his complete abandonment by all his associates except his father Henry Gatz, showing the emptiness of his social connections

He rents a small house next to the enormous mansion of the mysterious Jay Gatsby, who hosts elaborate parties with hundreds of guests. Nick visits his second cousin Daisy Buchanan and her wealthy husband Tom at their East Egg mansion, where tensions emerge over Tom's affair with Myrtle Wilson. Jordan Baker reveals that Gatsby and Daisy were lovers five years ago in Louisville, and Gatsby has bought his mansion specifically to be near her. Nick arranges a reunion, and after an initially awkward tea meeting, Gatsby and Daisy reconnect joyfully. The narrator reveals Gatsby's true identity: James Gatz from North Dakota, who reinvented himself at seventeen when he met the wealthy Dan Cody.

On the hottest day of summer, tensions explode when Gatsby and Tom fight over Daisy in New York City. Though Daisy initially declares her love for Gatsby, she cannot deny that she once loved Tom, shattering Gatsby's dream of recreating their past exactly as it was. Returning from the city, tragedy strikes when Daisy, driving Gatsby's yellow car, hits and kills Myrtle Wilson who rushes into the road thinking Tom is driving. George Wilson, devastated by his wife's death and believing the driver was also her lover, seeks vengeance. After a night of grief-stricken vigil with Michaelis, Wilson disappears and tracks down Gatsby, shooting him dead while he floats in his pool before killing himself.

Nick becomes solely responsible for Gatsby's funeral arrangements as everyone abandons him. Klipspringer calls only to retrieve tennis shoes, Wolfshiem refuses to attend despite their criminal partnership, and Daisy and Tom flee without a forwarding address. The funeral procession consists of just Nick, Gatz, the minister, servants, and unexpectedly, the owl-eyed man from the library. Before leaving, he confronts Tom, who reveals he told Wilson that Gatsby owned the car, and has a final conversation with Jordan Baker. Nick reflects on the story as fundamentally about the West and the corruption of the American Dream, ending with Gatsby's belief in the green light and the tragic impossibility of recapturing the past.

people.name: Nick Carraway
people.description: The narrator, a young Midwesterner who moves to West Egg in 1922 to learn the bond business. Yale graduate, WWI veteran. Becomes the sole person responsible for Gatsby's funeral arrangements and affairs after his death. Disgusted by the moral emptiness of the wealthy elite, he decides to return home to the Midwest. Before leaving, he confronts Tom about his role in Wilson's revenge and has a final conversation with Jordan. Reflects on the story as fundamentally about the West and realizes he and the others were all Westerners subtly unadaptable to Eastern life. At thirty, he claims to be 'five years too old to lie to myself and call it honour.'

people.name: Jay Gatsby
people.description: Nick's mysterious wealthy neighbor whose real name is James Gatz from North Dakota. Shot and killed by George Wilson while floating in his pool. His father reveals his childhood ambition through a boyhood copy of Hopalong Cassidy containing a detailed self-improvement schedule from 1906. Wolfshiem describes finding him as a destitute young major after the war, eating four dollars' worth of food and needing work. His funeral is attended by only a handful of people despite the hundreds who came to his parties. Represents the corruption of the American Dream and the impossibility of recapturing the past.

people.name: Daisy Buchanan
people.description: Nick's second cousin, married to Tom. The first 'nice' girl Gatsby ever knew, whom he met as a young officer at Camp Taylor in Louisville. After the accident that kills Myrtle Wilson, she flees with Tom, taking baggage and leaving no forwarding address. Completely abandons Gatsby after his death - sends no message or flower to his funeral. Represents the careless wealthy elite who smash up things and people then retreat into their money.

people.name: Tom Buchanan
people.description: Daisy's wealthy husband, former Yale football star. After Myrtle Wilson's death, he flees with Daisy. When confronted by Nick on Fifth Avenue, he reveals he told George Wilson that Gatsby owned the car when Wilson came to their door armed with a revolver. Justifies his actions by claiming Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and that he suffered too, crying over dog biscuits in Myrtle's apartment. Represents the carelessness of the wealthy elite who let others clean up their messes.

people.name: Jordan Baker
people.description: Athletic young woman, famous golf champion and friend of Daisy's. Has a final conversation with Nick before he leaves the East. Claims to be engaged to another man and accuses Nick of throwing her over on the telephone. References their earlier conversation about bad drivers, saying she 'met another bad driver' in Nick, whom she had thought was honest and straightforward. Their relationship ends definitively with Nick feeling 'angry, and half in love with her, and tremendously sorry.'

people.name: George Wilson
people.description: Owner of a garage in the valley of ashes. Myrtle's husband who discovered her affair and locked her upstairs before her death. After Myrtle's death, he tracked down Tom and Daisy's house, armed with a revolver and threatening to kill Tom unless he revealed who owned the yellow car. Tom told him it was Gatsby to save himself. Wilson then found and murdered Gatsby before killing himself. His quest for vengeance was driven by grief and the belief that the car's owner was also his wife's lover.

people.name: Myrtle Wilson
people.description: Tom Buchanan's mistress, wife of garage owner George Wilson. Killed when she runs into the road toward Gatsby's yellow car, thinking Tom is driving. Tom later cries over the dog biscuits he bought for her, found in their apartment after her death. Her death sets in motion the tragic chain of events that leads to Gatsby's murder.

people.name: Henry C. Gatz
people.description: Gatsby's father from Minnesota who arrives for the funeral. Shows Nick a photograph of Gatsby's mansion that his son had sent him, and reveals Gatsby's childhood copy of Hopalong Cassidy containing an ambitious self-improvement schedule from 1906. Describes how Gatsby bought him a house and was generous after becoming successful. Reluctant to put away mementos of his son, believing Jimmy was 'bound to get ahead' and always focused on improving his mind. Represents authentic parental love and Midwestern values.

people.name: Meyer Wolfshiem
people.description: Gatsby's business associate involved in criminal activities. When Nick visits his office at 'The Swastika Holding Company,' Wolfshiem initially hides but then emerges to share memories of first meeting Gatsby - a destitute young major who ate four dollars' worth of food and needed work. Claims he 'made' Gatsby and 'raised him up out of nothing.' Despite their close partnership, refuses to attend the funeral, saying he never likes to get 'mixed up' when someone gets killed. His abandonment reveals the shallow, self-serving nature of their criminal relationship.

people.name: Klipspringer
people.description: Frequent guest at Gatsby's parties who practically lived at the mansion. Calls Nick after Gatsby's death but only to retrieve a pair of tennis shoes he left behind, claiming he needs them for a picnic in Greenwich. Shows no interest in the funeral, representing the selfish, superficial nature of Gatsby's party guests who enjoyed his hospitality but abandoned him in death.

people.name: Owl-eyed Man
people.description: The man Nick found marveling over Gatsby's books in the library three months earlier. Appears unexpectedly at Gatsby's funeral, the only party guest to attend. Removes his rain-soaked glasses to see the grave and comments on how hundreds used to attend Gatsby's parties. Calls Gatsby 'the poor son-of-a-bitch,' showing genuine sympathy. Represents the rare person who saw through to Gatsby's essential humanity.

people.name: Dan Cody
people.description: Wealthy yacht owner who became Gatsby's mentor and 'best friend.' His photograph hangs over Gatsby's desk. Represents Gatsby's transformation from James Gatz and his introduction to wealth and the wealthy lifestyle that shaped his dreams.

people.name: Stella
people.description: Meyer Wolfshiem's secretary at 'The Swastika Holding Company' who initially tries to prevent Nick from seeing Wolfshiem, claiming he's in Chicago. Scrutinizes Nick with 'black hostile eyes' and becomes indignant when he insists on seeing her boss.

people.name: Lutheran Minister
people.description: Clergyman from Flushing who arrives to conduct Gatsby's funeral service. Glances at his watch anxiously as no mourners arrive, and Nick asks him to wait an extra half hour, though no one comes.

places.name: West Egg
places.description: Less fashionable of two egg-shaped land formations on Long Island, where Nick rents a small house and Gatsby owns his mansion. Site of Gatsby's death and funeral. Nick describes it in his dreams as 'a night scene by El Greco' with grotesque houses under a sullen sky. Gatsby's house remains empty after his death, with grass grown long and an obscene word scrawled on the steps that Nick erases before leaving.

places.name: East Egg
places.description: More fashionable area across the bay from West Egg, where Tom and Daisy Buchanan live. The site from which they fled after the tragedy. Nick recalls driving Gatsby and Daisy there the night of the accident, a memory that haunts taxi drivers who point out Gatsby's gate.

places.name: The Cemetery
places.description: Final resting place where Gatsby is buried in a thick drizzle. The funeral procession consists of only a motor hearse, limousine with Nick, Gatz and the minister, and Gatsby's station wagon with servants and the postman. The owl-eyed man arrives unexpectedly, and someone murmurs 'Blessed are the dead that the rain falls on' as Gatsby is laid to rest.

places.name: The Swastika Holding Company
places.description: Meyer Wolfshiem's business office in New York where Nick goes to ask him to attend Gatsby's funeral. Located behind a door that initially appears empty, with Wolfshiem hiding behind a partition before reluctantly agreeing to see Nick. The site of Wolfshiem's final abandonment of Gatsby.

places.name: Fifth Avenue
places.description: New York street where Nick encounters Tom Buchanan walking in his 'alert, aggressive way' in late October. Tom stops to look in a jewelry store window before confronting Nick and revealing his role in directing Wilson to Gatsby. The site of their final confrontation where Tom justifies his actions.

places.name: Union Station
places.description: Chicago train station that Nick remembers from his youth, where Midwestern college students would gather at Christmas time before taking the train home. Represents Nick's nostalgia for the authentic Midwest and his realization that this has been 'a story of the West, after all.'

places.name: The Midwest
places.description: Nick's homeland, which he describes not as wheat and prairies but as 'the thrilling returning trains of my youth, and the street lamps and sleigh bells in the frosty dark.' Represents authentic American values in contrast to the corrupt East. Nick realizes he, Tom, Daisy, Jordan and Gatsby were all Westerners who possessed 'some deficiency in common which made us subtly unadaptable to Eastern life.'

places.name: Louisville
places.description: Kentucky city where Daisy grew up and first met Gatsby when he was a young officer in 1917. The site of their original romance that Gatsby spent his life trying to recapture.

places.name: The Beach
places.description: Shore area where Nick goes on his final night, sprawling on the sand and contemplating the 'old island here that flowered once for Dutch sailors' eyes—a fresh, green breast of the new world.' The site of his final meditation on Gatsby's dream, the green light, and the American Dream itself.

open_threads.description: Nick's moral awakening and disgust with the wealthy elite's behavior, leading to his complete break with Jordan and his sole responsibility for Gatsby's funeral as everyone else abandons him
The funeral arrangements for Gatsby and the revelation of his complete abandonment by all his associates except his father Henry Gatz, showing the emptiness of his social connections

The revelation of Gatsby's true origins and character through his father's mementos - the photograph of the mansion and the Hopalong Cassidy book with its ambitious self-improvement schedule from his youth
Nick's final reckoning with the East Coast elite through his confrontations with Tom and Jordan, learning the full extent of their carelessness and moral bankruptcy
Nick's philosophical reflection on the story as fundamentally about the West versus East, the corruption of the American Dream, and Gatsby's impossible quest to recapture the past
